hook: You already know that networks and hierarchies are everywhere — from your computer's file system to Google Maps giving you directions. But how does software actually *navigate* these structures so quickly and reliably? This unit reveals the elegant tree-based algorithms that power real-world tools you use every day.
concept: This unit explores how trees — a special family of graphs with no cycles — serve as both a modeling tool and an algorithmic workhorse for organizing data, connecting networks efficiently, and finding optimal paths. The unifying idea is the *rooted, weighted tree* as a structure that turns complex network problems into tractable computations.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to identify, draw, and reason about rooted and binary trees, including key vocabulary like parent, child, leaf, height, and level that underpins every topic ahead.
Master the three systematic ways — preorder, inorder, and postorder — to visit every node in a binary tree, a skill directly used in compilers, expression evaluation, and search algorithms.
Apply Breadth-First Search and Depth-First Search to extract a spanning tree from any connected graph, building the foundation for both network design and pathfinding.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Minimum Spanning Trees: Kruskal's and Prim's Algorithms
topic_roadmap.description: Use Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms to find the least-cost way to connect all nodes in a weighted graph — the same problem faced by engineers designing road, power, and communication networks.
Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to compute the shortest path between two nodes in a weighted graph, exactly as GPS and routing software do millions of times per day.
Analyze how efficiently the algorithms in this unit scale by expressing their running times using Big-O notation, giving you the language to compare and critique algorithms like a computer scientist.
